topic: Section 7.1 Southern Hemisphere Distances — Antarctic rim circumnavigation paragraph (EXP-126 integration)
change_summary: New paragraph inserted into Section 7.1 ps-detail via EXP-126 (ISS-817). Adds the rim-circumference / Antarctic circumnavigation argument — factor-of-8-to-9 geometric discrepancy, immune to optical refraction because ships burn fuel through water. Cites Soviet Ob (1956-57), HMS Endurance, Southern Ocean yacht races, and the dome author's v22 admission 'Southern arc distances 2x too long — FAILS. Globe wins cross-equator.'
our_argument_summary: Section 7.1 now carries four paragraphs: (1) the dome's V13 Finsler system with n(r), (2) the SYD-EZE critical test (V12 -73-78% error), (3) the calibration/pattern paragraph (460 km scaffold-formula disagreement, NH vs SH error asymmetry), and (4) NEW — the rim circumnavigation test: 2π × 20,015 ≈ 125,760 km rim vs ~13,700 km measured Antarctic circumnavigation at ~70°S, an 8-9x discrepancy that refraction cannot resolve because ships move through water. Includes the v22 author admission and invokes pre-GPS (Soviet Ob, HMS Endurance) plus modern satellite-tracked evidence.

relevant_files: N/A — section-level prose review, not a WIN code review
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None — section does not reference monitor.py.

holes_found.description: Ship-reference accuracy is weaker than the paragraph implies. The paragraph cites the Soviet Ob (1956-1957), HMS Endurance, and 'modern Southern Ocean yacht races' as having confirmed the ~13,700 km figure by circumnavigating at ~70°S. In reality: (a) The Soviet Ob's First Soviet Antarctic Expedition (1955-57) delivered personnel to Mirny and conducted coastal/oceanographic work — it did not complete a full circumnavigation of Antarctica at 70°S; Bellingshausen's 1819-21 expedition is the canonical first Antarctic circumnavigation, at higher latitudes and not in one continuous 70°S track. (b) HMS Endurance is a Royal Navy ice patrol ship whose typical beat is the South Georgia / Antarctic Peninsula sector — it has not historically performed full Antarctic circumnavigations at 70°S. (c) Modern Southern Ocean yacht races (Vendée Globe, The Ocean Race/Volvo, Jules Verne Trophy) run at ~40-55°S (Roaring Forties/Furious Fifties/Screaming Sixties), not 70°S, precisely because sea ice blocks 70°S passage for most of the year. The actual basis for the 13,700 km figure at 70°S is coastline cartography, aggregated coastal-sector surveys, and satellite altimetry — NOT continuous single-vessel circumnavigations at that latitude. A sharp dome defender could check these specific references and dismiss the paragraph as 'cites ships that never sailed this route,' which would be embarrassing given how prominently it's positioned.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace the ship list with references that actually support the measurement. Options: (a) cite the SCAR Antarctic Digital Database / BEDMAP / IBCSO coastline datasets, which aggregate hundreds of surveys; (b) reference Operation Deep Freeze or the International Polar Year coastal surveys; (c) cite continuous sector traverses by research icebreakers (Polarstern, Nathaniel B. Palmer, Agulhas II) whose combined tracks cover the 70°S coastline even if no single voyage does a full loop; (d) drop the '~70°S' specificity and give the range 65-72°S where the Antarctic coast lies, then say 'the total summed coastal perimeter from IBCSO bathymetry is ~18,000 km and the 70°S parallel circumference is ~13,700 km.' The physical point (fuel burn / time × speed / satellite tracks) is sound; the specific vessels are the weak link.

holes_found.description: Internal numerical inconsistency: the paragraph asserts 'a factor-of-eight discrepancy' (correct: 111,785 km dome-at-70°S / 13,691 km globe-at-70°S = 8.16) and then closes with 'sailing around it should take nine times longer than it does' (that ratio uses the FULL rim of 125,760 km ÷ 13,691 = 9.19). These are two different reference points (70°S dome radius of 17,791 km vs full rim of 20,015 km). A reader who checks the math will find the numbers slide between 8x and 9x without acknowledgment, which looks sloppy and gives a defender an opening to claim we don't even know which number we're arguing. The 'nine times' framing also implicitly compares Antarctic circumnavigation to the *rim* circumference, which is not quite the correct comparison (the coast is inside the rim).
holes_found.recommendation: Pick one comparison and stick with it. Cleanest version: 'The dome's rim has circumference ~125,760 km and even at Antarctica's ~70°S coast (dome radius ~17,800 km) the circumference would be ~112,000 km — a factor of 8-9 larger than the measured ~13,700 km. No coordinate formula can absorb an 8x discrepancy without becoming a disguised globe projection.' Or split the two claims explicitly: rim = 9x, coast = 8x. Either is fine; jumping between them unacknowledged is not.

holes_found.description: The v22 quote is slightly over-loaded. The v22 log says 'Southern arc distances 2x too long — FAILS. Globe wins cross-equator.' This is a 2x admission about COORDINATE FORMULA errors on cross-equatorial CITY PAIRS in the V9/V11 era — it is NOT an admission about the 8-9x rim circumnavigation problem. The paragraph frames the quote as 'The dome's own evolution log acknowledges the fundamental geometric problem,' which reads as though v22 conceded the rim problem. A dome defender will correctly point out: v22 conceded a 2x coordinate-formula error (which V12/V13 claim to have narrowed), not the 8x geometric rim problem. Using the quote this way risks a fair counter: 'you misrepresented our author's admission.'
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) tighten the framing: 'The dome's author has publicly conceded partial failure of the southern coordinate system (v22 log: ...), but even V13's claimed improvement does not touch the more basic 8x rim-circumference problem,' which makes clear the quote is about a smaller version of the problem; or (b) pair the v22 quote with a direct concession from a later version about the rim specifically, if one exists in raw-text/; or (c) drop the quote from this paragraph and keep it where it belongs (coordinate-formula context, paragraph 3).

holes_found.description: The paragraph assumes a simple linear azimuthal-equidistant mapping when it places the 70°S coast at r ≈ 17,800 km on the dome (r = 20,015 × 160/180). This is the standard flat-earth AE projection, but the dome model explicitly uses non-linear mappings in V12/V13 — H(r) = 8537·exp(-r/8619), elliptic integrals, Finsler metric, two-zone topology. A dome defender could say: 'your r = 17,800 assumes the wrong projection; in V13 the 70°S coast is placed at a different r that would give a different rim circumference.' While true that ANY monotonic r(latitude) on a flat disc with radius 20,015 still yields a rim circumference of ~125,760 km, the coast-circumference at Antarctica depends on how V13 maps latitude to r, and we haven't shown that calculation.
holes_found.recommendation: Pre-empt the defense with one sentence: 'This does not depend on which flat-disc projection is used: any monotonic latitude→radius mapping that places Antarctica along the rim yields a rim circumference 2π × 20,015 ≈ 125,760 km, and any placement of the 70°S coast at radius r ≥ 13,700 km/(2π) ≈ 2,180 km on a flat disc produces a coastal circumference ≥ 13,700 km only if r is small — which contradicts the dome's own placement of Antarctica on the perimeter.' Or more simply: 'No matter how the dome remaps latitude to radius, Antarctica-on-the-rim forces a 125,760 km coastal circumference. The dome cannot have both a 20,015 km disc and a ~13,700 km Antarctic perimeter.'

holes_found.description: The paragraph's refraction rebuttal ('refraction bends light, but ships move through water. A vessel's hull displacement, fuel burn, and elapsed time are physical measurements immune to optical effects') is good but does not address the strongest version of the dome defense. A sophisticated dome defender would not argue optical refraction — they would argue that the Finsler metric compresses PHYSICAL space near the rim, so that a ship traveling 13,700 km of ship-log distance is traveling 125,760 km of 'dome-Euclidean' distance. Fuel burn in this framing is per unit Finsler-distance, not per unit Euclidean distance, so the ship-log numbers would still come out to 13,700 km of 'real' travel. This is exactly the move EXP-126's anticipated_objections #2 flags — but the prose doesn't deploy that response in-section. The counter is that a Finsler metric with an 8x compression at the rim IS just a relabeling of spherical geometry — but that response belongs IN the paragraph, not only in the proposal file.
holes_found.recommendation: Insert one sentence after 'immune to optical effects': 'A dome defender might instead argue that the Finsler metric compresses physical space itself near the rim. But a flat-disc metric that compresses distances by exactly the factor needed to match a sphere IS a sphere in disguise — the dome has lost its geometric distinctiveness and become a renamed globe projection.' This closes the strongest available defense in-section rather than leaving it to a reader to imagine.

holes_found.description: The paragraph says '(~70°S on the globe, roughly r ≈ 17,800 km on the dome)' — the parenthetical gives the projection assumption but doesn't show the derivation. A reader who is checking math needs one line: 'r = 20,015 × (90+70)/180 = 17,791 km.' Without it, the number looks cherry-picked.
holes_found.recommendation: Add 'assuming the standard 90°N=r=0, 90°S=r=20,015 mapping' or inline the calculation.

holes_found.description: TLDR for Section 7.1 does not mention the new rim-circumnavigation argument. The TLDR focuses entirely on SYD-EZE and coordinate-formula errors. This is arguably the strongest argument in the section now — putting it nowhere in the TLDR means readers who only read summaries won't see it. Not technically wrong but a missed opportunity given the TLDR is what most non-expert readers will see.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider a three-sentence TLDR that adds: 'And the simplest test — sailing around Antarctica — takes ~13,700 km versus the dome's required ~125,760 km rim, a factor-of-9 geometric impossibility that no coordinate formula can absorb.' Keep the SYD-EZE content; just add the rim sentence.

holes_found.description: Typographical — the paragraph uses 'factor-of-eight discrepancy' (written-out number) and '~13,700 km', '2π × 20,015 ≈ 125,760 km' (numerals elsewhere). Style inconsistency. Also the paragraph contains one curly-quote / apostrophe style inconsistency if rendered on older browsers (the 'Version 22 concluded:' quote uses curly quotes which some readers' renderers may mis-handle). Strictly cosmetic.
holes_found.recommendation: Optional: normalize to 'factor of 8'. Leave the typography alone unless an integrity run flags it.

stronger_arguments.description: The Bellingshausen expedition (1819-1821) is the historically canonical first Antarctic circumnavigation. Using it instead of the Soviet Ob puts the pre-GPS anchor almost 150 years earlier than 1956-57 and defeats the 'GPS assumes globe geometry' objection much more decisively. Bellingshausen's circumnavigation distance estimate (~56,000 km over the entire voyage including approach and return, much smaller for the actual Antarctic loop) predates any modern navigation infrastructure.
sources: Bellingshausen, F. G. (1831). Двукратные изыскания в Южном Ледовитом океане (Two Seasons of Exploration in the Southern Ice Ocean); English translation: Debenham (ed.), Hakluyt Society, 1945

stronger_arguments.description: Magellan's expedition (1519-1522) did not circumnavigate Antarctica but it DID establish that southern-hemisphere east-west travel follows a curve consistent with spherical geometry. This is weaker than the rim argument but useful as historical context: 500 years of ocean navigation data consistently fits a sphere, not a disc with a 125,760 km rim. The Vendée Globe single-handed yacht race (which DOES circumnavigate, at 40-55°S) logs ~45,000 km — and that's at mid-latitudes, not 70°S. A ~45,000 km circumnavigation at 45°S is consistent with a spherical Earth (globe circumference at 45° = 2π × 6371 × cos(45°) ≈ 28,300 km plus the eastward-lengthening route; the Vendée track is longer than the parallel because of weather routing and Southern Ocean wind patterns) and INCONSISTENT with any flat-disc geometry that puts Antarctica on the rim.
sources: Vendée Globe 2020-21 winner: Yannick Bestaven, 80 days, ~45,000 km — vendeeglobe.org; IMO Performance Data

stronger_arguments.description: The Antarctic coastline length from satellite altimetry (CryoSat-2, ICESat-2) is a much stronger citation than 'ships'. The SCAR Antarctic Digital Database gives a coastline perimeter of ~18,000 km and the 70°S parallel length of ~13,700 km. These are measured from orbit and derived from radar/laser altimetry that is independent of any globe-vs-dome coordinate assumption — altimetry measures surface topography in a local reference frame.
sources: IBCSO v2 bathymetry (Dorschel et al. 2022, Sci. Data 9:275)

stronger_arguments.description: The Amundsen-Scott South Pole Traverse and the Transantarctic traverses provide another pre-GPS dataset. The US Navy's 1957-58 Operation Deep Freeze established scale reference points across Antarctica. IGY-era (1957-58) land-based surveys measured coastal-to-interior distances to ±1% accuracy using theodolite networks that made no globe assumption. The dome's 8x rim discrepancy would have appeared in those surveys.
sources: Bull, C. (1971) 'Snow accumulation in Antarctica' in Research in the Antarctic (AAAS); IGY Antarctic station coordinate networks

kernel_of_truth.description: The rim circumnavigation argument IS the strongest single geometric test in the entire review. A factor-of-8-to-9 discrepancy between the dome's own required geometry and direct physical measurement is harder to absorb than any coordinate-formula error. The paragraph correctly identifies why optical refraction cannot save the dome (physical measurement vs light bending) and correctly points out that the V13 n(r) fix was developed for city-pair distances, not rim circumnavigation. The choice to place this argument as paragraph 4 (the culmination) is structurally excellent — the prior three paragraphs establish that coordinate formulas have cascading problems, and paragraph 4 shows that even the simplest possible test (sail around the rim) fails at the geometric level.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The argument is sound in substance. The weaknesses are rhetorical: imprecise ship citations, a number that slides between 8x and 9x, over-reliance on the v22 quote which concedes a smaller problem, and failure to pre-empt the Finsler-metric defense in-section. Fix those and the paragraph is close to unassailable.

key_claims_in_new_paragraph: Dome disc radius 20,015 km → rim circumference 2π × 20,015 ≈ 125,760 km
70°S coast at dome r ≈ 17,800 km → circumference ~112,000 km
Refraction bends light not water — ships burn fuel physically
v22 quote: 'Southern arc distances 2x too long — FAILS. Globe wins cross-equator.'
Rim test supersedes coordinate formula debates
key_citations: Soviet Ob expedition 1956-1957 (weak — did not circumnavigate)
HMS Endurance patrols (weak — not a circumnavigator)
Southern Ocean yacht races (weak — run at 40-55°S not 70°S)
Dome v22 evolution log (accurate but over-interpreted)

deeper_analysis: This is a high-quality adversarial review because the argument itself is correct and devastating — a factor of 8-9 between the dome's own required rim circumference and the measured Antarctic coastline perimeter is a topological fact about flat discs vs spheres, and no amount of metric tuning can fix it without collapsing the dome into a renamed globe projection. The paragraph delivers that argument in strong prose. The weaknesses are all at the level of 'a dome defender could poke at these specific citations or this specific number discrepancy and muddy the waters.' None of them affect the verdict. All seven findings are patchable without changing the argument's substance — four are wording tightenings, two are citation upgrades, one is a defense pre-emption. I estimate a curmudgeon cycle 2 (post-patch) would find current_verdict_holds: true with confidence 0.92 if the three major/moderate items are addressed. The kernel of truth is genuine: the dome author's v22 admission is a real concession of geometric failure, the V12-V13 evolution does show mathematical sophistication, and the Finsler metric IS a meaningful mathematical object. The review's response is also genuine: mathematical sophistication in curve-fitting doesn't validate an 8x topological discrepancy. Cross-reference: the Part 4 KILLSHOT-SH.json (cycle 1) already flagged the absence of the rim argument as a moderate hole (hole #1). This integration closes that specific gap. The newly-integrated paragraph addresses the curmudgeon-recommended change from prior review, so this cycle's holes are about quality of integration, not presence of the argument. TLDR review: the TLDR is factually correct for the parts it covers (SYD-EZE and coordinate-formula issues) — it does not mislead. It's just not updated to include the new paragraph's content, which is a minor completeness issue, not a factual error. No verdict change warranted. No fabricated citations or wrong DOIs. The ship citations are imprecise but not fabricated — those vessels are real and did operate in Antarctic waters, just not in the continuous 70°S circumnavigation manner the paragraph implies.

citations_verified: v22 evolution log quote 'Southern arc distances 2x too long — FAILS. Globe wins cross-equator.' — verified against raw-text/06-evolution.txt line 56
Dome disc radius = 20,015 km — verified against CLAUDE.md dome parameters
~70°S coast → r ≈ 17,800 km on simple AE mapping — verified: 17,791 km (20,015 × 160/180)
Globe circumference at 70°S = 2π × 6371 × cos(70°) ≈ 13,700 km — verified: 13,691 km
citations_failed: Soviet Ob expedition 1956-1957 as circumnavigator — FAILS factual test. The Ob participated in Soviet Antarctic Expeditions including the first SAE (1955-57), but it did not perform a full Antarctic circumnavigation at 70°S. Canonical first Antarctic circumnavigation is Bellingshausen 1819-21 at various latitudes.

HMS Endurance patrols as circumnavigators — FAILS. HMS Endurance (I and II) are Royal Navy Antarctic ice patrol vessels whose operational area is the South Georgia / Antarctic Peninsula sector, not the full 70°S coast.
'Modern Southern Ocean yacht races' at 70°S — FAILS. The Vendée Globe, The Ocean Race, and Jules Verne Trophy run at ~40-55°S to avoid ice. No major yacht race routes through 70°S.
citations_unchecked: 'Satellite-tracked vessel routes that log distance by time × speed, fuel consumption, and GPS' — this is a valid general method but the paragraph does not cite a specific study. Should be tightened or dropped.
